speaker: interviewer
text: So you're at a train station and just said goodbye to your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we took off in a train. And the train went to-- we went to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's we?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some other-- there was some other children. I guess the-- the train originated in Berlin. There must have been people from Berlin. I was-- I-- I guess maybe about 30. 30 children-- different parts of Germany, you know, Frankfurt. We continued on, on to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. All Jewish kids. You know. We went to Vienna, and we stayed a few days in Vienna in the Jewish Community Center, which, in those days, was still-- [INAUDIBLE] Austria was annexed to Germany. It was already in Germany. Somehow, it was a little more-- Jewish life was still-- was still existing-- under restrictions, but it was still there in-- in Vienna. And then we, I remember, a few days, we continued on, the same group.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On the train. And then-- and then, later on to the border, to the border town. I forgot the name-- name of the town. And then we got off. And we had to climb over the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria, Germany, you know, and Yugoslavia. OK. It was-- was a cold, wet night. Raining and everything else. And I guess, on the other side, on the Yugoslavian side, there was some-- some people with some trucks. Or-- I remember even some-- some farmers with-- with horse-- horse and buggies, I guess, you know, with horse cart. There were-- probably were being paid. And they took us-- they took us to Zagreb, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-A-G-R-E-B, which was the-- was the capital of Croatia, you know, in Yugoslavia. It consisted of several states-- Croatia, Slovenia, Serbia. Zagreb was Croatia. All I remember is I arrived in Zagreb, and I was-- I was very sick. Very sick. So immediately-- they-- they couldn't put me to the hospital because, you know, the hospital was all-- all Catholic hospitals. You know, who is he? Where does he come from?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me to a Jewish family. I remember the name was Levi. And they called a doctor. And later on, I found out that I had-- I had contracted pleurisy. And I was about, I would say, I was an inch away from being declared dead, in the sense that-- the-- whatever it was, almost infected-- something-- pleurisy is something in the ribs. I think it was a sickness. How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleurisy, P-L-E-U-R-I-C-Y, something like that. Pleurisy. It was a real infection. And I was there. So they nurtured me-- they nurtured me back to life. OK. In the meantime, the original group that I came-- left for Palestine. I don't know who they are. I remember some names from when-- when I was with them-- a couple of kids was-- some other kids from my town. Today I have no idea who they were. So they are now-- I was left-- I was left in this Jewish family in Zagreb.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names, the Levis?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember their names. They were a very nice Jewish family by the name of Levi. That's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay with the Levis?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six months. It took me that-- that long to-- to-- to recuperate. And then-- by that time, the Nazis occupied Yugoslavia. So here we are again, German occupation of Yugoslavia. And especially in Croatia, they found some willing helpers. They were-- they were called the Ustase. Their leader was a fellow by the name of Dr. Ante Pavelic. These were real Croatian Nazis, collaborators. Like-- Like-- You've heard the expression, 'the quislings,' the collaborators-- these were the Croatian Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that leader's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-V-L-E-I-C-H, Pavelic. I think that was his name. You know, he was-- he got-- I understand, after the war, they hanged him with all the rest of them. Real-- real-- Yeah, real-- real Nazis, probably thugs. I don't know what kind of a doctor he was. And so we were there in this-- in this in the Jewish community in Leipzig-- I mean, in Zagreb. And we were-- in the meantime, I met other children who arrived later from Germany-- Berlin, Frankfurt, you name it. I just remember there was one girl from Leipzig. And she was-- they were there. And we were finally together in the Jewish Community Center. And we started to organize ourselves into a group. We found one man who was-- he was a Yugoslavian Jew. Thank God he's still alive. His name is-- it was [INAUDIBLE]. Today's it's Josef Itaj. He lives in Israel. He was one of the leaders. And then we had some other-- some other people-- a fellow by the name of Marko Silberschaltz who died, a great fellow by the name of Dr.-- Professor Boris Jochvidson. He was from Russia. And so we-- we decided to organize ourselves and figured we have to do something. In the meantime, the-- the-- the decree came out that all Jews have to wear the badge, a yellow badge with a 'z' on it, which means [? Zidov. ?] OK. [? Zidov. ?] And I remember one instance where, first of all, I went to the-- we went to the German-- to the German army headquarters. We were figuring, we were Germans. We were German citizens. See the army, the army was not the SS. You know, you could-- you could go to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went to the army, you know, and asked them, do we have to be wear this? I mean, we are German citizens. We are not Croatian citizens. I mean, we had that much gall. I think today-- I mean, we must have been nuts. But they didn't-- he says, nope. No, you're not-- you don't have to wear it. You're not-- you're German citizens. And my friend reminded me of something-- that on this desk was a bunch of stamps. You know-- stamps? And I understand I stole. I forgot already. I stole one of those stamps and put in my pocket. And later on the group was able to use this stamp to-- to validate some phony papers. And-- At my 60th birthday party, which I had, one of my-- one of the guys that was with me reminded me of that. I had forgotten already. Anyhow, the other instance I remember-- I once went to the-- I went to the post office. And there was this big Ustase. And he asked me, you know, in Croatian, he says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? You know, do you have-- do you have identification?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had this German paper, which was-- was inside was plainly marked. First of all, I had a big 'J' on it, Jude. And then I had my name as Arnold Israel Wininger. All Jewish males in Germany had to have the middle name Israel, and the women had Sarah. They want to make sure that, in case your name is John Smith, that your name is John Israel Smith. They know that you're a-- you're a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the outside, it had the swastika and the eagle and everything else, and said Deutsches Reich, you know, Germany, and everything else. So I pulled that thing out quick. And I said to them in a real German accent, like I, can't you see that I'm a member of the German police? And this guy clicked his heels and greeted me with, Heil Hitler, you know, and this kind of stuff, and let me go. I will never forget that. This was real chutzpah, and I was lucky. So then we-- then they organized. They organized. I really had no part of it. I didn't know. I was helpless. I didn't know what to do. Who was they?

speaker: interviewee
text: These-- these-- these adult leaders, you know, who also emigrated. But they knew their way around. And somehow, they procured-- we procured papers us to get out of Croatia, trying to get into Slovenia. Now, Slovenia was occupied by the Italian army. OK. I guess Mussolini must have told Hitler, give me a piece of the action. So he gave him, and says, you take Slovenia, and I take the rest. Slovenia was occupied by the Italians. And we found out that the Italians were [INAUDIBLE]. They were somehow-- They were-- they weren't going to kill us or something like that. They're a little more [? menschen. ?] So this Professor Boris Jochvidson who-- bless his memory-- he was a real aristocratic Jew from Russia. He was-- he came from Tiflis in Georgia. He was associate professor, associate conductor of the Berlin Philharmonic or the Berlin Opera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-C-H-V-E-D-S-O-N. Professor Boris Jochvidson. And I'll come back to this man later on. He spoke Italian. Being, you know, being associated with opera and everything else, you have to know Italian. Plus this fellow Marko Silberschaltz, he also-- he-- he was a-- I don't know what he did, but he-- he spoke Italian. He could get by in Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they organized this whole thing. And one day, we just took the train and left for Ljubljana. Now, Ljubljana was the capital of Slovenia. We had papers to get out of the German-occupied part of Yugoslavia, which was Croatia, but we had no papers to enter the Italian part of Yugoslavia, Slovenia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this I'll never forget. So we reach the German post. There was-- The Germans weren't even there. It was the Croatians. You know, they-- they didn't know-- we had these German documents. We all were Germans, and they must have told him we're a family of German soldiers, stuff like that. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got out of Germany. Here we are now in no man's land. The Italian police comes in. I'll never forget. Fascists. Black shirts. Real-- like-- like the Italian SS, you know, so to speak. No comparison, but big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess Professor Boris Jochvidson and Marko told them point blank, these are Jewish orphans. Their parents have been killed in the concentration camps. We have a place in the mountains of Slovenia where to go. We won't bother anybody. Please help us. This is the thing they told us. So these-- the Italian-- the Italian military police and these all were high-ranking officers. And he said, wait. I guess they had to go back and, you know, talk to Rome or speak to the general in charge. I don't know what it was. But it was about 20 minutes. I'll never forget. We did not know what going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many kids were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's about 30. It was about 30 kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old was I? This was 1941-- '42-- '41/'42. I was-- '26, '36, I was, maybe, about 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the range of the ages of  the kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: The range of ages was from-- from-- I think, from 10-- from 10 to-- the-- the children were from 10 to-- 10 to 20. And then we had about three or four adults with us. We came-- so after 20 minutes, we didn't know what was going to happen to us. They came back. And they said, avante, go forward. I'll never forget that word-- avante. In Italian, it means, go ahead. Ah, what a relief that was. You know, from this day, I loved it. And when we arrived in Ljubljana, there was the Italian Red Cross at the railroad station with milk and bread and everything else-- feeding these Jewish kids. This was before Stalingrad. This was before El Alamein. When they Nazis and the Axis were riding high, there was the Italian Red Cross helping Jewish kids to give them some food and blankets and everything else. From that day on, I learned to love the Italians in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we proceeded then to this-- we had this-- it was an old castle. It was called Lesno Brdo, L-S-N-O B-R-D-O. It was up in the mountains. Somehow the-- I don't know how they got it, but the Jewish agency was able to get this one particular godforsaken place up in the mountains. And there we set up a [? hashara. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Our-- our aim was, eventually, still to get-- get to Palestine. You know, Israel was not-- not even thought of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what is [? hashara? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Explain what?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is [? harshara? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hashara ?] is preparation for, you know, for the kibbutz or the moshav. You know, learn-- learn things-- I think the word means preparation, if I'm-- if I'm correct in-- my-- my Hebrew is not that good. But we were-- we were studying Zionism, Jewish history, even some skills, agriculture, you know, to know in case-- if we could reach Palestine, if we are in a kibbutz or yishuv or-- or a moshav-- the different types of moshav-- you know, the kibbutz and the different types of kibbutzim-- and-- and Zionist history, and everything else, among other things. Plus we tried to get some general education. So we set up a [? harasha ?] in-- in the mountains of Yugoslavia. In Slovenia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the [? harshara? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Lesno Brdo. You know, we had no name for it. You know, we just-- we organized ourselves in the little [INAUDIBLE]. And we just-- we just made-- made the best of it, you know, tried to live. Before we know-- things started to get hot. What-- what-- what I mean by getting hot-- the Tito Partisans, the Communists, you know, started to get active. And they are-- they are ones, the Italian-- the-- the Italian army-- which was very good to us-- as a matter of fact, there was a time the-- the officers came-- came up with a-- you know, and they wanted to have the music and dance because Professor Boris Jochvidson played the piano. And, you know, they wanted-- you know, wanted-- one of our fellows, he used to sing in the-- in the children's choir of Vienna, so he used to sing Italian operas. You know, so this guy has-- nostalgic. They used to come to us, and they were a little dancing with our girls. You know, we weren't-- they weren't little girls, but young girls. But they wanted a little-- they were homesick. So they're very nice to us. So we had the Italians on one side, supposedly our enemies. And then we had the Communist partisans on the other side, supposed to be our friends. We were afraid of them and not of the Italians. As a matter of fact, there's one episode I'll never forget. I almost got shot-- killed by the Communist partisans because one of my jobs was to go down to the village, pick up the mail, bring a milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while I was there, I-- I started to talk to the Italian soldiers. You know, I wanted to learn Italian and say, bon giorno, you know, good day. Come stai, how are you? Things like that. And that's how I learned Italian. And fortunately, today, I'm pretty good. I speak-- I think I speak a fluent Italian. And one day, I came back up there, and I see this-- one of these commandant, you know, one these commanders of the partisans, talking to one of our [INAUDIBLE], and called me over and said, well, what are you doing down there with the Italian soldiers? I said, nothing. I says, I'm just-- I'm just talking to them, you know, how are you-- I want to learn Italian. Aren't you telling him that we're up here? I says, no! I wouldn't tell him [INAUDIBLE]. What saved me-- and he had his revolver in his hand, you know, right in front of my nose. What saved me is that my [INAUDIBLE] said, this guy's stupid. You know, he wouldn't know one thing. Well, he's a kid. Well, what do you want from him? He didn't know. So that what's saved me. Otherwise, I wouldn't be here today to tell you the story. Do you remember that partisan's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. First of all, they all had just-- they all had names, you know, first names. And you know, you didn't-- all I knew was the-- I knew the code word. If you went-- if you went-- say you went down in the woods, and somebody, one of the partisans [INAUDIBLE] called out, stop, what's the code word? The code word was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It means death to Fascism, freedom to the party. And don't ask me to spell it. [LAUGHS] But that was the code word. We knew the code word. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you walked in, you got stopped. They knew-- if you knew the code word, this was fine. You know. But they-- we were very, very uncomfortable with them. Were the partisans organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very-- highly organized. But to-- to kill somebody, to shoot somebody-- was-- there was no problem, no questions asked. If you didn't like somebody, OK, for one reason or the other, you're a partisan, you could shoot and kill him. And you could always say he was a traitor or something like that. Nobody-- nobody bothered. Nobody asked. There was no questions asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many partisans were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, who knows? Could have been 50, 50 up there, heavily armed in the woods. And they were all under the command of Marshal Tito. OK. And they were-- they were fighting-- they were, you know, they were fighting the Italian army. And one time, I remember, as a matter of fact, we went to the farmers trying to get some-- in order to live, you know, I used to go to the farmers and, actually, sometimes beg. Beg for a piece of bread. We used to go in the woods collecting berries, blackberries, or mushrooms, you know, to live. And I remember one time, without knowing it, all of a sudden, I see-- myself-- I see German soldiers marching. Unbeknown to me and another friend, we had-- we-- we went back in the German-- the German part. OK. So we said to ourselves there that the best thing to was just be quiet. You know, we-- we just walked like we were farmer boys and went back to the Italian part. And the German soldiers didn't-- didn't even pay any attention to us. And they were just marching there. They weren't-- weren't on guard. But, you know, out in the fields, you just walk, and all of a sudden, you're in German territory. So I forgot that. I was lucky. [LAUGHS] Anyhow, so that's-- that's our life up in Lesno Brdo. And eventually-- eventually, we had to almost ask the Italian army to evacuate us from there. It was getting too dangerous for us to be there. [INAUDIBLE] there was shooting back and forth between the Italian army. We were in the middle, and we were kids. OK. And we really asked the Italian army to-- to-- to get us out. Now, these are things which people have told me because I was not involved in the negotiations. I just-- And eventually, the Italians made us civilian prisoners of war. That's what they [? called us. ?] We practically-- and they-- they sent us to central Italy, in a little village called Nonantola. Nonantola is in the province of Modena. A Jewish fellow, at one time, owned this big villa. But it was abandoned. And now, through the Jewish, the Italian Jewish community, somehow we were able to get this place so that the-- the Italians would send us to this particular village. And I'll tell you a little bit more about that village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we practically had to escape from so-called 'our friends,' the partisans-- you know, I mean, trying to get over and which-- which put ourselves under Italian protection. I mean, it's odd. So there-- there we-- Why did the Italians agree to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? There ?] [? was ?] the Italians over there-- the Italians really, in my opinion, never wanted any part of this war. The Italians are very good-natured people. They saw these kids there, the children there, orphans, and they just had [INAUDIBLE], like I say. They had pity. You know, and they understood. And so they-- they-- they agreed to help us. This is the Italians. And I must only say that many times I think the real role of Italy in saving Jews has never been told fully. I mean, we are just a small-- a small chapter in this, in the role that Italy played. I'm sure there's other-- other instances there, too. But the Italians were very good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you reached to that castle--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So we-- there, here we are now in Italy. And I'll never forget when I first-- when we first crossed into Italy-- you know, train-- we went by train-- they-- they stopped at certain stations, and I looked around. This was already in Italy. I looked around, and I looked at all these people. And I said to myself, if the-- if the Germans ever come down here, they're gonna shoot everybody. They all look Jewish. You know, they all had this Mediterranean look. You know, sometimes-- sometimes you really cannot tell if somebody's an Italian or Jewish. They have all these similarities. So we-- we-- we-- we arrive in Nonantola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-O-N-A-N-T-O-L-A. And it's the province of Modena, M-O-D-E-N-A. By the way, Pavarotti comes from Modena, so that's his hometown. It's near-- it's near-- not-- not too far from Bologna. Bologna is better known. You know. But this is Pavarotti's hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrive in this village, which is about, I would say, maybe 15 miles away from-- from Modena. We see practically the whole village is at the railroad station, you know, with the-- they only had two policeman, three policemen, you know, the chief of police, he was called the [? carabinieri ?], which is the militia. And the whole village-- so we-- we were wondering, why, what's this-- what's this big welcome? We found out later on that the village came-- they had never seen a Jew. The word came around in this little-- little village that Jews are coming, just like like-- like saying-- saying like, we are gonna have people from Mars coming. They had never seen a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: This whole village came to the railroad station to look. They figured, maybe we have horns, maybe-- I don't know-- OK. That's-- that's the stories. Now we are in Nonantola, and again, we are setting up [? hashara. ?] In the meantime, we get other-- other people are joining us from other-- we had some Yugoslavian kids. The group, almost-- from 30, we almost were 100 setting up shop in-- in this-- in this place, in Nonantola. According to the law, we were-- we were not supposed to leave the city, the village, but all we had to do is go to the chief of police and tell them that we wanted to go into town, which was Modena. Which was, Modena was the town. And he just gave us a-- just gave us a-- it was called [INAUDIBLE], You know, let's-- give him of those cards. Go ahead. They really didn't care. I have to say that the village of Nonantola deserves-- no, deserves a great place in the Jewish history. This whole village became our friends, especially two people-- Dr. Giuseppe, Morealli, he was the town doctor, and a priest, Don Arrigo Beccari. These people befriended us, and later on helped us, counseled us. And both of them became righteous Gentiles in Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can you spell their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Dr. Giuseppe G-I-U-S-E-P-P-E M-O-R-E-A-L-I, Morealli. And the other one is Don, D-O-N, A-- double-R-- I-G-O. Dr. Morealli died. Even though they both were incarcerated by the Nazis-- you knwo, but they were later let go. Dr. Morealli, I understand, died later on. Don Arrigo Beccari is still alive. I saw him-- I saw him in 1900 and-- at Robbie's wedding in 1992. I mean, the same as my wife, [INAUDIBLE]. And there was a reunion last year in Nonantola. And unfortunately, I couldn't go to-- to see him. There was a great reunion. I'm still friendly with his son, Dr. Morealli's son. He's the town doctor. We call each other on Christmas and New Year's and send cards. I know his wife, and he's got a-- he's got a child. He's got, I think, two sons, grandchildren, so it's a great relationship between us and the community-- the community of Nonantola.

words.text: he's got a-- he's got a

speaker: interviewer
text: Going to stop here and pick up with  the next tape.
